summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'solstice'?

Solstice comes from Latin 'sol' (sun) + 'sistere' (to stand still) — the Sun appears to pause at its extreme north or south position before reversing direction. The summer solstice is the longest day; the winter solstice is the shortest.

What is the 'heliacal rising' of a star?

Heliacal rising was crucial for ancient calendars. The heliacal rising of Sirius (the brightest star) was so important to Egyptians that they based their calendar year on the approximately 365-day interval between successive heliacal risings.

What is a 'cardinal direction'?

The cardinal directions are the four main points of the compass. Ancient builders aligned structures to these directions using astronomical observations — for example, tracking where the Sun rises and sets at the equinoxes (due east and west).

What is the 'Dresden Codex'?

The Dresden Codex is one of only four surviving pre-Columbian Maya books. It contains accurate Venus tables, eclipse prediction tables, and ritual almanacs, demonstrating the Maya's advanced mathematical and astronomical knowledge.

What is an 'equinox'?

Equinox means 'equal night.' The vernal (spring) equinox and autumnal (fall) equinox each occur once per year. At equinox, the Sun rises due east and sets due west, which was used by ancient builders for alignment.

What is a 'star catalog'?

Star catalogs have been compiled since ancient times. Hipparchus (Greek, ~130 BCE) created one of the first comprehensive catalogs of about 850 stars. Ancient Chinese and Indian astronomers also maintained detailed catalogs.

What is a 'lunar calendar'?

Many ancient civilizations used lunar calendars because the Moon's phases are easy to observe. A lunar month is about 29.5 days, giving a 354-day year (12 months). This is shorter than a solar year, requiring periodic adjustments.

What is a 'zenith' in astronomy?

The zenith is the highest point in the sky for an observer — straight up. Ancient cultures tracked when the Sun reached the zenith (at tropical latitudes) as a significant astronomical event. The opposite point, directly below through the Earth, is called the nadir.

What is a 'megalithic structure'?

Megalithic (mega = large, lithic = stone) structures include Stonehenge, Newgrange, and Carnac. Many show alignments to solstices, equinoxes, or lunar standstills, indicating their builders had sophisticated astronomical knowledge.

A student asks: 'The Maya predicted eclipses without telescopes. How is that possible?' What is the best explanation?

Eclipse cycles repeat with mathematical regularity. The Saros cycle (~18 years, 11 days) produces similar eclipses. Over centuries of careful observation and record-keeping, the Maya identified these patterns and could predict when eclipses would occur.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Multiple ancient cultures independently developed methods to predict eclipses. What does this convergence suggest about humanity's relationship with the sky?

Independent development of eclipse prediction by Babylonians, Maya, Chinese, and Greeks shows that tracking the sky was a universal human drive. The practical value (calendars, agriculture, navigation) and spiritual significance of celestial events motivated this knowledge in every major civilization.
